text:
Investigation 4

For Every 100: Introducing Percent

In this unit, we have represented comparison statements in different ways to answer questions. In this investigation, we will learn another way to represent comparison statements.

Sometimes we want a convenient way to think about comparisons and to compare comparison statements. One way is to write equivalent ratios using 100.

For example, in the Green neighborhood:

The number of voters in favor of dress codes for middle school students is :

31 out of 100 or 31:100.

This is a part-to-whole ratio comparison. A part-to-whole comparison that uses the quantity 100 as the whole is called a percent. The word percent means "out of 100." Percentages can help us with comparisons because we will have the same-size quantity in the whole each time

31:100 or 31 percent of the voters favor a dress code for middle school students.

or

31% of voters favor dress codes.

or

The percentage of voters who favor dress codes is 31% .

Did You Know?

In a similar way to how percent can be loosely translated to "per cent" o :per 100," the English language uses the prefix centi- for hundredth or cent- for hundred to start words that have a relationship with 100. We even use the word cent to describe the value pf a penny since there are 100 cents in $1.

The centi- and cent- prefixes can be useful in understanding a relationship with 100; for example, 1 centimeter = 0.01 meters, and 1 century = 100 years. The Spanish language also uses the prefixes centi- and cent-, with centimeter being translated as centimetro and century being translates as, centuria.

In American Sign Language, the sign for percent looks like the "%" symbol. Using an "o" hand, the sign involves a fast "Z" motion. To learn more, you can search for "percent in American sign language" on your choice of video streaming sites.
